sentences.text: Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The next-- the next day, when I came out of this hiding from the garage, there was all of the dead people laying in the-- in the streets in the ghetto.
And they announced that tomorrow, it has to be judenfrei-- what hides-- that no Jews are allowed there.
So--
The Germans came into the Judenrat.
And they said this-- everybody has to go out.
They're bringing us to Tarnopol, they said.
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] tomorrow, everybody has to be, the next day-- it was the third day after this-- you have to all be in the center of the ghetto there, on the Sammelpunkt.
And you have-- you'll go to Tarnopol.
But we didn't trust the-- the Jews didn't trust them that it is so.
Whoever could go in hiding somewhere was going.
And a lot that-- that wasn't a way how to go out, but they tried.
And as I mentioned, before, my husband's brother-in-law had a mill from-- oh, no.
I have to go back to that.
They-- they took them to the-- they said they're going to Tarnopol.
But instead, they took them to [NON-ENGLISH], and they had to dig their own graves.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they killed them all-- which I have a picture that they made the monuments later.
[CRYING] And this was the liquidation.
But in this time, at this mill, we had a bunker built.
And it was like we had to go into it in hiding.
We decided, some people-- it was about 40, 50 people.
And they are elderly people and young people.
We went into this bunker under the mill.
And one of those-- I didn't want to go no more.
I said, I have enough of this life.
I'm going to Tarnopol.
But they persuaded me so that I shall go into the bunker.
And I went with them.
A girlfriend and my cousin.
So we-- to go into this bunker, you had to go through-- through-- swim through like a water, because the mill was on the water.
And there, we had to walk out in there.
We were hiding there.
And one of-- one of those girls knew a Volksdeutsch who said that he will help us to go out of the ghetto.
It was still surrounded, the ghetto.
All around were those collaborators who helped them, and the Germans.
Germans less, more of the collaborators.
But they all were with guns.
They said we couldn't go out of the ghetto.
So we went into hiding there.
And under mill was a small bunker, too-- where two people could hide.
And we didn't let this Volksdeutsch-- Pascht was his name-- he took ransom money for it and jewelry that he-- that he helped us to go out.
But we didn't tell them that where our bunker is.
Only he knew about those two people on top of the mill, where it is.
And he used to come in and give us signals whenever he could.
And we remained there for two weeks, because it wasn't possible to walk out.
In this time, when we were in the ghettos, we had to-- to eat something-- I mean, in the bunker in the ghetto.
We came in-- we used to walk out, a few of the younger people, and search the ghetto if there still was something.
Sometimes you found something to eat.
And one time, I walked out, and I found a jar of borscht.
And I brought it into this bunker.
And everybody had a sip of it.
And they-- [CRYING] until today, I don't remember that I had such a-- such a good, finer liqueur like this was.
The people in there were screaming.
Some got nervous breaks.
We were put to [INAUDIBLE] there.
And finally, we were out of the ghetto.
And there was one day that he came, and he gave us this-- he told us, today you can go out.
Yeah, this Pascht.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So when we walked out in groups, everybody had something.
My husband's sister, with her-- her husband and two children, they were the owners of this mill.
They had-- by a farm, or some better place that they all go in hiding.
So they went there.
And [SIGHS] some other people had a few places that they went out, mostly were killed because they didn't have where to go.
And they went in in the fields, and this-- me and my cousin and-- another-- a cousin girl, older than I was, and a cousin boy.
He was nine years old.
The three of us walked out, and we went back to Zawalow.
We didn't know where we shall turn, what we shall do.
But we had, like I mentioned before, a grocery store.
And we had-- there are people who helped us in the time.
They were owners of a restaurant.
And I came-- and we were very good friends with her.
I came up.
We came there to her.
And we asked for hiding.
But she refused.
She let us stay there a day, and we couldn't remain there.
We had to leave, so we went, like-- my father owned land.
We had, like, a piece of land.
And I knew where it was.
And I use-- we said, we'll go over there.
And we make ourself a bunker, and we hide there.
And we went there.
We didn't know-- there was, like, a forest, too, in this land, like one acre  of forest.
Yeah, the three of us.
No.
And I went-- we were trying to-- to make a bunker, but we didn't have nothing there.
So a neighbor, a Gentile neighbor that we used to live before, where we had to leave the house-- and he had also land there.
I went over to them, and I asked maybe he can give us something that we shall try to make us a bunker.
He brought us shovels and food.
And we tried, but we couldn't accomplish nothing.
So we left there and we went to another village.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] To this other-- oh, yeah.
This village, we came, I knew it was--
Yes, Seredny.
My father used to deal a lot with them, with those people there.
And there were, like, nice people there.
And we figured we'll be able to find some hiding place.
But the village was in a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] low.
In a valley, yeah.
In a valley.
And we-- and we went into the fields in a-- on a hill.
So everybody saw us from the-- from the valley there.

And there was one-- as I mentioned, that they are nice people there who was helping them, the people there, because there were already groups in the forest that were hiding, like partisans, or what, from the ghetto.
Some young people didn't want to go into the ghetto.
They were hiding in the forest there.
And he was helping them.
Kefert-- Ivan Kefert.
And he saw that we were there.
He went over to the-- he came at night to us and told us that he'll bring us over to this group there.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So he came.
And we went in to our group, where I was-- [SIGHS] there was 39 people there in the place, in the forest.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
And there was other three groups of those-- there was a lot of groups that were doing this and hide like this.
I came into this group where we were 39 people.
And we were there for-- for the two weeks, maybe.
And then we-- we used to go out for food and-- whenever we could, but mostly the boys used to bring in.
And at night, we were watching there around the boys.
And this, early in the mornings, we used to go out to bring some water that we shall be able to-- to have drinking water, to cook something at night.
We used to cook something, like we used to take potatoes from the fields or other things.
And at night, we used to-- to cook them.
So I was-- when they-- two boys went out to get some water.
And we heard, like, a shot, a scream.
And one boy came back.
And the other didn't come back.
So we-- we felt that something is wrong.
And everybody got together.
And we start planning what we shall do, what-- maybe we shall be discovered there.
And we were surrounded.
Like, in the middle of the day, we were trying to walk around to this forest and see if there is a way to walk out.
But there wasn't.
In the middle of the day, came up two-- two men with [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with-- no guns, or how you call this?
These were the SS men?
Nazis?
No, no.
They were-- they said they were partisans.
But they were Ukrainian or Poles.
And they said, we came to-- to save you.
We wanted to-- we want to give you advice how to go.
We are partisans, and you'll be working with us.
If you want to come, and the-- the children, whoever is there, they'll be able to be safe, the elderly.
So we didn't think it is the truth.
But we didn't have no other choice.
So this time, they came in, and then another two came in.
And they started to plan how they will do with us, that first, they will take the men out, give them like directions, and what they have to do, how they have to-- to deal.
They will go and will do, like, sabotaging the trains, other things, you know, to-- to help them work in the partisans.
But it wasn't true.
They just made us believe.
And I don't know, that-- some of us didn't believe.
We felt that it is something there wrong.
But there was intelligent people, like lawyers, doctors, and just some people.
And we said-- they said to us, you have to do this way.
And we-- we even told them-- like, I remember they told them, there is another group there.
We want them, too, they shall come, if with the partisans to be.
Which was very wrong.
It's a good thing that we didn't know exactly where it is, because otherwise they would tell them, and they would find those other people, too.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
So they brought us to-- first they took the men.
And then they took the-- the women with the children, the younger men.
They-- they said, we're going to positions where we have to go and do something.
You will do this.
You-- all kind of plans they made out.
But everything was false.
They took away the men, and they probably killed them.
We didn't hear nothing.
Not probably, but for sure.
We knew-- but they said, we have to take the men separate in this.
And then at last remained only, like, about 20 people, let's say.
They took first the young and the stronger ones, and they killed them.
Then they took the rest-- it was already night-- and brought us to a-- it was in the forest, like a hunting house or what, that those who started the forest used to live there.
They brought us into a room like this.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when we were walking in the beginning-- so there were-- in the other group, there was a man who was the leader from those groups.
His name was Silber.
And he take-- he took them.
He came over there just to check out what we have to do.
And he found out that we are surrounded.
They had, like, a grenade and-- but they didn't do nothing.
I don't know for what reason.
If they would have throw it or something-- they-- they just took us there to this hunting house.
And they told them still that they are partisans.
I don't know if he believed.
He probably didn't believe.
They took him-- they took them out.
And we didn't know-- two-- two people, and again two people.
We didn't hear nothing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Later on, they started to-- when there was no more-- only like 12 people there, so they came in and they said-- my-- my cousin was near the door.
And they said-- they took her out.
Oh, she was about 25.
Brenda.
I have her in the picture there.
So when they took her out, we heard a scream-- screaming.
So we knew that something is wrong.
And then I said, I-- now when they'll come in-- and they locked the door again-- when they come, in I'll tell them that I'm going next because I don't want to no more hear and see what is happening.
When they came back, so they said-- I was right by the door-- by the door, so they pushed me back.
And I-- they didn't take me out.
But they said, [RUSSIAN] in Russian, I shall lay down.
So I laid down on the floor with my face down like this.
And I thought he was gonna shot me like this.
He was with a gun.
But they said, the rest shall lay down.
So on one side, on my right side, my little cousin from nine years laid down.
And he put his head on my neck.
[CRYING] They asked the rest people to lay down.
[CRYING] When they were all laying on the floor, [CRYING] my-- my husband's sister was there.
She was with two children-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] five and seven years old, two boys, such beautiful boys.
[CRYING] And she was, like, hiding them under her.
And they were all killed.
They start to shoot them, one after the other one.
And I don't know, I was laying there on the floor.
[CRYING] But I-- I didn't feel that I am shot, nothing.
If I was unconscious or what, I don't know what it was.
But I heard every shot.
[CRYING] And then they started [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to pull us out, outside, by our legs, to pull out-- outside.
It was in the middle of the night, dark.

And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] when everybody was out from there, [SOBBING] they took-- they started searching us because some of us had always something like sewed in, and some kind of jewelry or money or whatever.

Because in the time from the war, so we always-- like my father used to say, we shall-- everybody shall have something in case we can-- if we get catched, then we shall have something for ransom money.
[CRYING]
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] searched everyone.
And I don't know-- I was thinking of if I wanted to know if somebody is still alive or not.
I was trying to hear if somebody will say something or not.
But there was a girl who gave a cough.
And they said, somebody is living there.
So he bring her head-- and they were [SOBBING] tapping her like [CRYING] you hear in the butcher store.
[SOBBING] So I decided not to say that I'm alive.
And I wonder if they'll put me alive into the grave or what.
I didn't know what they will do.
And-- [CRYING]
And they had already a grave with those other people somewhere because they came with a wagon-- a big wagon-- [CRYING] and they put us in the-- in this wagon.
They-- I was the first one put in the wagon.
And the wagon had like such white-- it had like a ladder.
Yeah.
And I was the first one in the ladder-- on the wagon And then they put all the others on top of me.
[CRYING] They brought us then to this grave.
They were pulling us with the wagon.
[CRYING] I don't know how far it was, but they pulled the wagon.
They brought us there, this place.
[CRYING] And then they start throwing in the people into the grave.
I was full of blood from the other people.
[SOBBING] And then I was at last-- the last one.
So they still-- like they were playing with me.
One catched me by my legs, and one by my arms.
[CRYING] And they just throwed me like this, and I fell into the grave.
[CRYING] They just throw in a few shovels of dirt.
And they left like this-- because it was soon dawn.
[CRYING]
